---
title: "A Multilingual Corpus of Climate Finance Literature, 1990--2024"
author:
  - name: "Minh Ha-Duong"
    orcid: "0000-0003-2398-9009"
    affiliation:
      - name: "CNRS"
      - name: "CIRED"
    email: "haduong@centre-cired.fr"
date: "2026-03-20"
metadata-files: [data-paper-vars.yml]
bibliography: bibliography/main.bib
abstract: |
  This data paper describes a multilingual, deduplicated corpus of
  {{< meta corpus_total >}} works on climate finance published between 1990
  and 2024, assembled from {{< meta corpus_sources >}} bibliographic sources:
  OpenAlex, ISTEX, bibCNRS, SciSpace, institutional grey literature,
  and a curated teaching canon. The search strategy targets eight languages
  through a four-tier keyword taxonomy. A six-flag quality pipeline
  combining metadata checks, citation isolation, semantic outlier
  filtering, and cross-encoder relevance scoring produces a curated
  dataset with full audit trail. An internal citation network of
  {{< meta cite_refined_rows >}} links, extracted via Crossref and
  OpenAlex, supports co-citation
  analysis and bibliographic coupling within the field. Pre-computed
  384-dimensional multilingual sentence-transformer embeddings enable
  clustering, topic modelling, and cross-lingual analysis without
  re-encoding. The corpus, embeddings, citation network, and audit log
  are deposited in Zenodo under CC BY 4.0.
keywords: |
  climate finance -- bibliometric corpus -- multilingual --
  sentence-transformer embeddings -- scientometrics -- history of economic thought
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
---

<!-- Target journal: Research Data Journal for the Humanities and Social Sciences (RDJ4HSS) -->
<!-- Format: Data Paper, max 2,500 words, diamond OA, IISG/Openjournals -->
<!-- Structure follows Petram & Kruizinga (2024) exemplar: Introduction, Method, Data, Concluding Remarks -->

- Related dataset: "A Multilingual Corpus of Climate Finance Literature, 1990--2024" with DOI [10.5281/zenodo.19097045](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19097045) in repository "Zenodo"

## 1. Introduction {#sec-introduction}

Climate finance---the financial flows directed at mitigating and adapting to climate change---has become one of the most politically salient objects in international economic governance. The \$100 billion annual commitment made at Copenhagen in 2009, and the \$300 billion New Collective Quantified Goal agreed at Baku in 2024, are contested not only in their adequacy but in their very definition: what counts as climate finance depends on accounting conventions that are themselves disputed [@roberts_weikmans2017; @michaelowa2007].

A growing scholarly literature addresses this contested object, spanning economics, political science, international relations, development studies, and environmental governance. Yet researchers face a fragmented bibliographic terrain. Academic publications are scattered across databases with different coverage profiles. Institutional reports from the OECD, UNFCCC, World Bank, and Climate Policy Initiative, documents that have shaped the very categories of the debate, are absent from standard academic databases. The literature is also multilingual: while English dominates, French, Chinese, Japanese, and German traditions contribute perspectives that monolingual corpora miss entirely.

Recent bibliometric work has begun to map this literature. @care_weber2023 survey sustainable finance bibliometrics using Scopus; @shang_jin2023 analyse climate finance publications from Web of Science; @maria_etal2023 map green finance using Scopus with network analysis. While valuable, these studies rely on a single database each and cover English-language publications only. @shang_jin2023 lacks a data availability statement, @maria_etal2023 mentions a GitHub repository but provides no URL, and @care_weber2023 is behind a restrictive licence (CC-BY-NC-ND) with no public deposit. We verified these observations against each journal's data sharing policy and searched Zenodo, GitHub, and Dataverse for associated deposits.

Our dataset addresses these limitations by assembling a multilingual corpus from {{< meta corpus_sources >}} complementary sources, applying a documented quality-filtering pipeline with full audit trail, and providing pre-computed sentence-transformer embeddings that place all works in a shared semantic space regardless of language. The dataset was constructed to support a history-of-economic-thought study of how climate finance crystallised as a category of economic governance [Ha-Duong, under review]. However, its scope and documentation make it reusable for a range of applications across the humanities and social sciences: topic modelling, citation network analysis, bibliometric mapping, temporal analysis of concept emergence, and cross-lingual studies of how climate finance is discussed in different linguistic and institutional traditions.

## 2. Method {#sec-method}

### 2.1 Sources {#sec-sources}

The corpus assembles academic and grey literature from {{< meta corpus_sources >}} sources with complementary coverage profiles (@tbl-sources). Four are automated or semi-automated (reproducible from the scripts and an internet connection); two require manual export from restricted platforms.

| Source | Automation | Coverage |
|--------|------------|----------|
| OpenAlex | Automated (free API) | Primary academic source: tiered keyword search |
| ISTEX | Automated (public API) | French national archive (Springer, Elsevier, Wiley) |
| Grey literature | Hybrid (curated seed + World Bank API) | 16 curated reports (OECD, UNFCCC, CPI, UN) + World Bank repository |
| Teaching canon | AI-assisted (scraping + LLM extraction) | Syllabus readings from university courses worldwide |
| bibCNRS | Hand-harvested (CNRS credentials) | Non-English news & grey sources (Gale, Wanfang, NewsBank) in FR, ZH, JA, DE |
| SciSpace | AI-collected, hand-exported | SciSpace systematic review tool exports |

: Sources, automation level, and coverage scope. {#tbl-sources}

The search strategy uses a four-tier keyword taxonomy reflecting the evolving vocabulary of climate finance. Tier 1 consists of core terms in eight languages (English, French, Chinese, Japanese, German, Spanish, Portuguese, Arabic). Tier 2 covers institutional and diplomatic vocabulary (e.g., "clean development mechanism," "green climate fund"). Tiers 3 and 4 broaden the search to climate-adjacent terms, requiring concept-group co-occurrence filters (2-of-4 and 3-of-4 respectively) to maintain precision. The taxonomy was informed by keyword mining of {{< meta corpus_core >}} core papers (cited $\geq$ {{< meta corpus_core_threshold >}} times) and is defined in a version-controlled YAML configuration file.

The corpus is multilingual by design. While English dominates ({{< meta lang_english_pct >}}%), the strategy explicitly targeted French (*finance climat*, *finance climatique*), Chinese, Japanese, and German through ISTEX, bibCNRS, and multilingual OpenAlex queries. This deliberate multilingual scope, though it yields a modest non-English share, provides a foundation for cross-linguistic studies that monolingual corpora cannot support.

All API queries are bounded to publication years 1990--2024. Raw API responses are stored in an append-only pool as compressed JSONL files, preserving complete responses for future re-extraction without re-downloading. OpenAlex indexes RePEc, so economics working papers are covered without a separate harvest. The two hand-harvested sources (bibCNRS, SciSpace) together contribute 905 works before deduplication. bibCNRS is a CNRS legacy portal aggregating news and regional databases (Gale, Wanfang, NewsBank) that capture non-English public discourse on climate finance — a dimension absent from academic databases. Their inclusion is validated by multi-source overlap: many of their records are independently confirmed by automated sources, confirming retrieval consistency.

### 2.2 Data Structure {#sec-data-structure}

The merge pipeline combines records from all sources through two deduplication passes: DOI-based (normalised, lowercased) and title+year matching for records lacking DOIs. When duplicates are found, the maximum citation count is retained and metadata follows a source-priority order. Boolean `from_*` columns (one per source) track which databases contributed each record, enabling provenance analysis. The `source_count` field records multi-source agreement: of the {{< meta corpus_total >}} refined works, {{< meta corpus_multi_source >}} ({{< meta corpus_multi_source_pct >}}%) appear in multiple sources.

A six-flag filtering pipeline then evaluates every work. Three flags require no external data: (1) missing metadata (no title), (2) absent abstract combined with an irrelevant title (lacking domain-specific terms in any of the target languages), and (3) title blacklist matches (noise terms such as "blockchain" or "deep learning" without climate-finance context). Three flags depend on enrichment outputs: (4) citation isolation (pre-2020 works neither cited by nor citing any other corpus work), (5) semantic outlier detection (embedding distance from corpus centroid exceeding mean + 2 standard deviations), and (6) cross-encoder relevance scoring against the query "climate policy and financial mechanisms."

The pipeline removes flagged works unless they meet one of four protection criteria: citation count $\geq$ 50, presence in 2+ sources, within-corpus citations, or appearance in teaching syllabi. The teaching canon was built by scraping university course syllabi worldwide and extracting bibliographic references through a combination of DOI regex matching and LLM-assisted extraction, followed by fuzzy deduplication and CrossRef enrichment (details in the companion technical report). Users should note a structural harvesting bias: programmes using institutional learning management systems (Moodle, Brightspace, Canvas) rather than public-facing websites are not captured. Every inclusion/exclusion decision is recorded in the audit trail.

### 2.3 Quality, Completeness, and Potential Biases {#sec-quality}

@tbl-quality summarises corpus composition and metadata completeness per source. The "Refined" column counts works with a `from_*` provenance flag after deduplication and quality filtering; a single work may appear in multiple rows if discovered by several sources. %DOI is the share with a valid DOI, %Abstract the share with an abstract longer than 50 characters, and %Refs the share with at least one reference in the citation graph.

{{< include tables/tab_corpus_sources.md >}}

The merge pipeline reduces {{< meta corpus_raw >}} raw records to {{< meta corpus_total >}} refined works ({{< meta corpus_removal_pct >}}% removal rate). Citation graph verification against Crossref on a stratified sample of 30 papers yielded precision = 1.0 and recall = 1.0. The citation network contains {{< meta cite_refined_rows >}} rows covering 45% of corpus DOIs; coverage reaches 80% for core papers (cited $\geq$ 50).

Relevance filtering (flag 6) uses a cross-encoder reranker (BAAI/bge-reranker-v2-m3, 568M parameters) rather than a generative LLM, ensuring deterministic, reproducible scores without API costs. Calibration proceeded in two stages. First, we selected the best query from 100 candidates evaluated on a stratified sample of 200 weak-labeled papers (100 positive, 100 negative), achieving AUC = 0.766. Second, human validation on a separate stratified sample of 100 works (20 per score quintile, blinded to scores) yielded AUC = 0.818 and 81% accuracy (precision 74%, recall 76%); we coded an additional 100 boundary cases near the initial threshold to refine the final cutoff. An independent LLM audit provides a second check. These methods serve as independent checks on the flagging pipeline, not as ground truth, since what counts as "climate finance" is itself a contested category. The Zenodo deposit includes all calibration and validation tables.

Users should be aware of several potential biases. Non-English coverage remains limited ({{< meta lang_english_pct >}}% English). Citation counts reflect OpenAlex data as of the collection date and favour older, English-language works. Grey literature records often lack abstracts (though 97% have them in this corpus). The teaching-canon component reflects a harvesting bias toward business schools that publish syllabi on public websites; development economics and international relations programmes tend to use institutional platforms that are not publicly accessible.

## 3. Data {#sec-data}

- Climate finance corpus --- DOI: [10.5281/zenodo.19097045](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19097045)
- Temporal coverage: 1990--2024

The dataset consists of five files deposited in Zenodo, also reproducible from source via the pipeline scripts.

**refined_works.csv** ({{< meta corpus_total >}} rows). The primary corpus file. Each row is one deduplicated work with columns for: identifier, DOI, title, first author, year, abstract, citation count, publication venue, work type, language code, source provenance flags (one boolean column per source: `from_openalex`, `from_istex`, `from_bibcnrs`, `from_scispsace`, `from_grey`, `from_teaching`), a version-tracking flag (`in_v1`, marking works present in the v1.0 submission corpus), and quality-filtering metadata (`is_flagged`, `flag_reason`, `is_protected`). Of these, {{< meta corpus_core >}} core works (cited $\geq$ {{< meta corpus_core_threshold >}} times) form the field's most-cited core. @fig-bars shows the temporal distribution.

**embeddings.npz.** Compressed NumPy archive containing L2-normalised (unit-length) {{< meta emb_dimensions >}}-dimensional vectors for {{< meta corpus_with_embeddings >}} works, computed by the multilingual sentence-transformer *paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2*. The count exceeds the refined corpus size because embeddings are computed before quality filtering; the full cache supports alternative filtering strategies. Each vector encodes the concatenation of title, abstract (if longer than 20 characters), and keywords. The model maps texts in English, French, Chinese, Japanese, and German into a shared semantic space: non-English works cluster with thematically similar English-language works rather than forming language-specific clusters. These vectors serve as direct input for BERTopic, top2vec, or other embedding-based topic models, and support cosine-similarity search for finding related works.

**corpus_audit.csv.** Complete audit trail: every work receives a decision (`kept`, `removed`, or `protected`) with reasons.

**citations.csv.** Internal citation network ({{< meta cite_refined_rows >}} rows) extracted via Crossref and OpenAlex enrichment. Each row records a (citing, cited) pair where both works belong to the corpus, enabling co-citation analysis, bibliographic coupling, and citation genealogy studies.

**Source catalogs.** Individual per-source catalogs (`openalex_works.csv`, `istex_works.csv`, etc.) are preserved in the deposit, enabling provenance tracking back to individual databases before merge and deduplication.

**Backward compatibility with v1.0.** The `in_v1` boolean column marks works present in the v1.0 submission corpus (git tag `v1.0-submission`). Of the 29,875 v1.0 identifiers, 29,697 match the current corpus (99.4%); the 178 unmatched works reflect strengthened quality filters and improved deduplication. For exact replication of v1.0 results, retrieve the original files from the Zenodo deposit.

![Annual publication volume in the corpus. The search window is 1990--2024; the earliest works date from 1992.](figures/fig_bars.png){#fig-bars width=100%}

The dataset is released under CC BY 4.0. The pipeline is organised in three phases: corpus building (API harvesting, merging, enrichment, and filtering), analysis (embedding projection, clustering, and derived statistics), and document rendering. DVC (Data Version Control) tracks the pipeline DAG (directed acyclic graph) and ensures reproducibility. API queries use fixed year bounds (1990--2024), and raw responses are archived for re-extraction. The four automated or hybrid-automated sources can be re-harvested with an internet connection; the two restricted sources (bibCNRS, SciSpace) have their raw exports included in the Zenodo deposit.

## 4. Concluding Remarks {#sec-concluding}

By combining {{< meta corpus_sources >}} complementary sources into a curated, multilingual, and reproducible dataset spanning 35 years, this corpus fills a gap in climate finance scholarship. The {{< meta corpus_sources >}}-source design pairs the breadth of OpenAlex with coverage from French (ISTEX), non-English (bibCNRS), and institutional (grey literature) sources absent from single-database corpora. Pre-computed multilingual embeddings lower the barrier to semantic analysis, enabling researchers to study thematic structure, temporal evolution, and cross-lingual patterns without re-encoding the full corpus.

Several research directions can build on this dataset. First, it provides an empirical base for quantitative studies of climate finance as a scholarly field, including history-of-economics analyses of how economists, policy institutions, and financial actors co-produced this contested concept. Second, the provenance columns support methodological studies of how different databases define the boundaries of "climate finance," a question with implications for systematic review methodology in the social sciences. Third, the citation network supports genealogical analyses: tracing how foundational concepts (carbon markets, green bonds, adaptation finance) propagated across disciplinary communities. Finally, the 35-year temporal span enables diachronic analyses of concept emergence and field dynamics, from the early UNFCCC negotiations through the post-Paris proliferation of green finance instruments.

The provenance architecture, with boolean flags per source and a documented merge pipeline, makes it straightforward to add new databases or extend the temporal window while preserving backward compatibility.

## Data Availability {.unnumbered}

The dataset is deposited in Zenodo at <https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19097045> under CC BY 4.0. The deposit includes refined_works.csv, embeddings.npz, corpus_audit.csv, citations.csv, and per-source catalogs. Total download size is approximately 430 MB. Suggested citation:

> Ha-Duong, M. (2026). A Multilingual Corpus of Climate Finance Literature, 1990–2024 (Version 1.1) [Data set]. Zenodo. <https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19097045>

New versions will retain the same concept DOI; version-specific DOIs are minted automatically by Zenodo. The `in_v1` column in refined_works.csv enables backward compatibility with analyses performed on earlier versions.

## Code Availability {.unnumbered}

The pipeline source code is available at <https://github.com/MinhHaDuong/Oeconomia-Climate-finance> under the MIT licence. The repository includes all scripts, configuration files, and DVC pipeline definitions needed to reproduce the corpus from source APIs. Key software dependencies: Python $\geq$ 3.10, pandas, scikit-learn, sentence-transformers, and DVC (Data Version Control). The full dependency list is pinned in `pyproject.toml`.

## Technical Environment {.unnumbered}

API harvesting was last run on 2026-03-23. The pipeline was executed with Python 3.12.3, pandas 2.2, sentence-transformers 3.4 (model: *paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2*), and BAAI/bge-reranker-v2-m3 for relevance scoring. Corpus building (API harvesting, enrichment, and filtering) runs on a single workstation; no GPU is required for harvesting or filtering. Embedding computation benefits from a GPU but completes in under two hours on a consumer GPU (NVIDIA RTX A4000, 16 GB).

## Funding {.unnumbered}

This work was supported by CNRS (Centre National de la Recherche Scientifique) through the author's permanent research position at CIRED (Centre International de Recherche sur l'Environnement et le Développement). The OpenAlex Premium API was provided by OurResearch.

## Acknowledgements {.unnumbered}

The author thanks the anonymous reviewers for their feedback.

## Ethics and Competing Interests {.unnumbered}

This study uses only publicly available bibliographic metadata; no human subjects were involved. The author declares no competing interests.

## Author Contributions (CRediT) {.unnumbered}

**Minh Ha-Duong**: Conceptualisation, Methodology, Software, Data Curation, Validation, Writing — Original Draft, Writing — Review & Editing.

## References {.unnumbered}
